title: Migrate smart-router to async-iterator decision model and upgrade protobufs
preview:
Background: The current smart-router evaluates policy decisions in a synchronous, lock-heavy control path that exacerbates tail latency under bursty traffic and makes incremental changes (new fallback adapters and regional policy rules) risky. The protobuf contract for decision blobs is also split across two formats which complicates caching and cross-region fallback compatibility.

Goal: Replace the sync decision pipeline with an async-iterator based evaluation model that allows streaming policy decisions and progressive fallbacks (first-fast, then-complete), and standardize the decision protobuf to a single backward-compatible v5 schema. This reduces lock contention, short-circuits cold-paths, and simplifies retry/fallback logic.

Scope:
- Introduce AsyncDecisionIterator interface and migrate hotpath policy evaluators (affinity, capacity, SLA checks) to yield partial results.
- Implement a DecisionMux that composes partial decisions, emits earliest safe route, and continues to refine results for non-blocking fallbacks.
- Consolidate decision protobufs into decision_v5.proto with explicit versioning and compatibility shims for older workers.
- Update KV cache serialization for the new proto and add on-disk migration tools for cached entries.
- Add e2e and load tests to CI that exercise streaming decisions and regional failovers.

Trade-offs considered:
- Async iterator model increases code complexity in evaluators; mitigated by a small compatibility shim that allows gradual migration per-evaluator.
- Larger decision messages (while still compact) may increase network bytes; we will compress decision blobs in the cache and add heuristic TTLs.
- Rolling migration required careful cross-version compatibility; chosen approach is single forward-compatible proto + ordinal feature flags.

Progress updates: see comments for timeline and blockers.

Design artifacts and links:
- RFC (design doc): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Async+Decision+Model+RFC
- Proto spec: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ExampleProtoSpec
- Implementation PR (router core): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-router/pull/1428
- KV migration tool PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-tools/pull/89

Acceptance criteria:
- 99th percentile routing latency on synthetic burst test reduces by >= 20% on the hotpath compared to baseline.
- No increase in request failures during cross-region fallback tests.
- Cache migration completes without data loss in staging.
- All new CI tests pass and are gated on the PR.

Monitoring and dashboards:
- New dashboard: Console -> Routing -> AsyncDecision (runtime-1.26) with panels for decision latency histogram, partial-decision emission rate, and fallback invocation frequency.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge compatibility shims and proto v5 support behind a feature flag. Deploy to staging and run migration tool on staging KV.
2) Gradually enable async iterators per-evaluator (affinity first) using canary rows in the router.
3) Expand to 10% traffic, monitor 99p latency and error budget.
4) Full region-by-region rollout with automatic rollback on SLA breach.

Rollback steps:
- Disable feature flag to revert to sync pipeline immediately.
- If cache migration causes issues, restore KV snapshot from pre-migration backup and pause further rollouts.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Partial decision emission might lead to premature route selection that misses a better upstream option. Mitigation: DecisionMux applies safety predicates and waits for required evaluator classes for any high-cost routing decision.
- Risk: Cross-version incompatibilities. Mitigation: Proto v5 is designed with opt-in fields and a metadata header; all workers will accept v4 and v5 for 6 weeks during rollout.

Next steps:
- Finish DecisionMux integration tests (Mateo, 2026-03-05 to 2026-03-12).
- Run large-scale synthetic bursts in staging (Simone, 2026-03-15).
- Submit infra PR to enable compressed cache blobs in production (Priya, 2026-03-18).

title: Stride-aware watermark coalescer for mixed-length prompt/response streams
preview:
Background: Our continuous batching runtime currently struggles with mixed prompt/response workloads where sequences interleave short system prompts and long user responses. We observe two recurring issues: (1) KV cache residency spikes and fragmentation that cause out-of-memory (OOM) tail cases on smaller GPUs during spikes, and (2) suboptimal slot utilization because the scheduler either waits too long for long-turn arrivals or coalesces too aggressively, increasing latency for short-turn requests.

Goal: Design and ship a scheduler tuning that adaptively coalesces requests using a stride-aware watermarking strategy and coordinated KV stripe compaction to improve GPU memory utilization and reduce p95/p99 latency for tail-heavy mixed-length traffic while keeping median latency neutral or improved.

Key ideas: (1) Stride-aware watermarks: replace the single static windowing threshold with a set of watermarks keyed by observed stride (inter-arrival pattern and recent length histogram). Watermarks control how long a partially-filled slot will wait before emitting.
(2) Predictive prefetch elasticity: small online estimator that biases prefetch/KV warmup for slots likely to host long continuations; reduces cold-KV misses. Estimator uses exponential moving average of sequence-length-per-customer and recent turn lengths.
(3) KV stripe compaction/grooming: periodically compact KV fragments across slots that have transient short-lived contexts to reduce fragmentation and shrink residency. Compaction occurs under low-utilization and respects latency SLOs.
(4) GPU slab defragmenter integration: expose soft hints to the memory manager to evict or compress low-reuse KV shards (e.g., use fp16 quantization in-place) before admitting new long requests on memory-constrained devices.
(5) Graceful degrade policy: when predictive score indicates high probability of long-turn arrival but GPU reservation is low, fallback to routing to a compatible larger-variant or apply light quantization instead of stalling other short requests.

Why different from prior work: Instead of purely length-binned batching or fixed tail-hedging, this approach uses both inter-arrival (stride) signals and a light-weight online predictor to set per-cohort watermarks and couples that with KV-level grooming so memory and scheduling jointly improve tail behavior.

Metrics to track: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency, token throughput, average GPU active memory, KV cache miss rate, KV residency fragmentation metric, number of spill-to-disk events, percent of requests routed to larger-variants. Baseline window for comparison: last 7 days production mixed-turn traffic (sampled by region).

title: Stochastic ensemble calibration and post-quantization bias correction for E4M3/E5M2 on Hopper
preview: Enable a robust FP8 activation selection pipeline for Hopper (E4M3/E5M2) by combining multiple stochastic calibration seeds, an ensemble scoring layer, and a lightweight post-quantization bias-correction pass. This ticket addresses edge-case regressions observed in long-tail prompt families and conversational context drift when using single-seed calibration. The aim is to reduce worst-case accuracy drops while keeping latency impact within SLOs.

title: Customer-fidelity load generator for Private/VPC reproductions
preview: Build a reproducible, configurable load generator that can emulate customer traffic and environment characteristics for Private/VPC/on-prem Redwood deployments. The harness must produce stable, repeatable tokens/sec profiles and p95 latency distributions so we can attribute regressions to runtime/kernel/scheduling changes rather than test noise. Key features: replay of snapped request traces, synthetic traffic distributions (session lengths, user think-time, prompt prefixes), KV-cache warmup profiles, N-to-1 routing for multi-tenant simulations, network condition emulation (bandwidth, latency, jitter), and an automated regression detector that compares current runs to baseline runs and opens a ticket when drift exceeds thresholds. Outputs must integrate with Prometheus/Grafana and Redwood Console dashboards (token/sec, p95, p99, error-rate, cost-per-token).

title: Quantization matrix intake and SLA escrow for perf regression tracking
preview: Create a structured intake and validation flow for quantization profiles (INT8/FP8/GPTQ/AWQ) that ties each profile to a pinable model-version, publishes a standardized performance profile, and enrolls the artifact in an SLA-backed regression escrow. This ticket focuses on the intake contract, automated smoke checks, and the escrow mechanics that prevent accidental promotion of regressing artifacts.

title: Strata Shadow Allocator and Idle Migration playbook for KV fragmentation reduction
preview:
Problem: Continuous batching workloads create heterogeneous KV entry sizes and lifetimes. Over time this leads to interleaved small and large KV regions, internal fragmentation, and wasted GPU memory that reduces effective model capacity and forces more frequent costly fallbacks. Existing compaction/defrag approaches (epochal rewrites, eager slab coalescing) either cause high tail latency when triggered or require large pauses.

Proposal: Implement a Strata Shadow Allocator (SSA) plus Idle Migration playbook. SSA organizes the KV arena into lightweight strata driven by per-worker sequence-length histograms and recent access signatures. Instead of moving live active entries synchronously, we maintain a shadow indirection map that lets the allocator point hot lookups to a compacted target region while the real relocation happens opportunistically during idle GPU cycles. Parallel components: a residence score computed from access recency and batch-size correlation, a drift-correcting reshuffle policy (gamma threshold) that triggers micro-swaps only when projected free-byte gain exceeds relocation cost, and a relocation ledger for safe rollback and telemetry.

Why different: This design minimizes synchronous rewrite work by separating logical compaction (indirection updates) from physical memory movement, enabling small incremental background coalesces that exploit GPU low-utilization windows and avoid disrupting latency SLOs. It also introduces a short-lived shadow tier that absorbs churn from mixed-length requests and provides a graceful path for rollback if a relocation increases cache miss rate.

High-level goals: reduce measured "wasted_kv_bytes" by at least 40% under mixed-length RLHF-like workloads, keep 95th-percentile generation latency regression <= 2% during background migration, and enable safe live rollouts with telemetry-driven auto-tuning of gamma thresholds.

title: Route consistency window during peering promote causes transient resolver-binding drift
preview: Summary: During a peering promote (when a secondary peering session is promoted to primary to balance load or recover capacity), some Dedicated tenant routes can appear to be consistent at the control plane level while dataplane bindings (egress resolver affinity and next-hop for SNAT) lag by a small window (1-30s). The lag can cause tenant traffic to be forwarded to an egress path that no longer has the correct resolver mapping, producing transient DNS misresolution and connection resets for long-lived client sessions. Several customer escalations reported 5-30s spikes of 500-level proxy errors and DNS NXDOMAINs coincident with peering promotions during scheduled maintenance or autoscaler-driven reparent events.

title: Terraform KMS/HSM lifecycle operator for private deployments
preview: Build a Terraform-based lifecycle operator (module + orchestration patterns) for integrating KMS and HSM backends into Redwood Private deployments. The operator will provide idempotent provisioning, zero-downtime key rotation orchestration, IAM binding bootstrapping, and a safe path for migrating secrets between Secret Manager and Vault in air-gapped and VPC environments. This ticket covers the Terraform module design, integration tests, CI gates, and an upgrade strategy for existing on-prem clusters.

title: Regional retention routing matrix and customer blocklist
preview: Goal: Define and implement a canonical routing matrix that enforces EU data residency and retention constraints while supporting customer-scoped blocklists (blocklist = explicit customer/tenant prohibition for specific regions or tiers). This ticket covers schema, runtime enforcement points (API gateway, routing layer, and model-serving adapters), operator controls, and migration of existing customer mappings. We must support: 1) 'EU-only' customers (requests must stay inside EU regions), 2) residency-preferred customers (prefer EU but can failover under operator-approved conditions), 3) blocklisted customers that cannot be routed to specific tiers or regions, and 4) mapping to model variants for fallback (e.g., quantized local variant vs hosted large variant). This spec must be implementable in hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

title: Modernize CI build system: small base images, build cache, and toolchain bumps
preview:
Summary: Reduce CI build times, lower container image size/CVEs, and improve developer iteration speed by modernizing Docker base images, enabling persistent build caching, and upgrading linters/toolchains across service repos.

Background: Over the last 18 months our monorepo and multiple service repos have accumulated slow CI jobs, large Docker images (some >1.2GB), and stale toolchains (old Node, Go, Python images and linter versions). This increases developer cycle time, costs in GitHub Actions minutes and container storage, and surface area for CVEs.

Scope:
- Replace legacy base images (debian:buster, node:14-slim) with slim/pinned or distroless images where possible and publish SHA-digested images to GHCR.
- Standardize multistage Dockerfiles with BuildKit best-practices across services (cache mounts, stage ordering, node_modules caching).
- Enable remote build cache for GitHub Actions via registry-backed buildx cache or S3-backed BuildKit cache for large artifacts.
- Upgrade linters/toolchains: golangci-lint -> v1.54, Go -> 1.21, Node -> 20.x (with node:20-bullseye-slim or node:20-distroless), Python -> 3.11; adopt ruff for Python linting and update pre-commit configs.
- Add automated image size checks and Trivy scan step in pipeline; fail CI on new high/critical CVEs unless in a documented exception.

Non-goals: Rewriting build systems (no Bazel migration in this ticket), not touching on-prem private air-gapped deploy image distribution policies (separate EPIC).

Why now: We have recurring incidents of flaky CI due to transient network pulls and long rebuilds, and finance has flagged container storage as rising. Early adopter teams (Console and Runtime) reported 30–40% cold build time reductions with proof-of-concept changes done in ENG-4799. We should unify the approach.

Success metrics (measurable acceptance criteria):
- Median CI build time across primary services reduced by >=30% for cold builds and >=50% for warm builds (measured over a 2-week window).
- Average published image size reduced by >=25% for updated services.
- Remote build cache hit rate >=70% within 2 weeks of rollout.
- No new high/critical CVE introduced in updated base images (Trivy).
- Developer feedback: 80% of participating engineers report improved local iteration times in post-change survey.

Deliverables:
- CI template updates under .github/workflows/ci-build.yml and shared action at .github/actions/buildx-cache.
- Dockerfile templates and example service change PRs (runtime-api, console-web, model-loader).
- Lint/toolchain upgrade scripts and pre-commit config update.
- Rollout checklist and monitoring dashboard for build times, cache hit rate, and image sizes.

Risk/Trade-offs:
- Distroless images reduce attack surface and size but complicate debugging (no shell). Provide debug variants or sidecar debug images where required.
- Upgrading Go/Node may surface subtle build/test regressions; we will run canary upgrades per repo and keep old CI matrix entries until stable.
- Remote cache introduces operational complexity and potential consistency issues; we will provide conservative TTLs and fallbacks to registry pulls.

Notes about migration strategy: we will start with low-risk services (static frontends and internal tooling) then move to runtime-critical services (inference runtime, model loader). Each service migration is a small PR: Dockerfile changes + CI templating + linter bump.

Monitoring & observability: Add metrics to the CI orchestration layer (via GitHub Actions job timings exported to our Prometheus proxy) and publish dashboards in Console -> CI Performance.

Attachments and links are below in the links field.

title: Passive-region warmup validation and circuit-breaker probing
preview: Objective: Create and validate a deterministic warmup and probe regimen for passive regions to ensure fast activation with bounded risk. We will automate traffic-probing and circuit-breaker exercise sequences, verify backup/restore integrity, and exercise load-shedding rules under controlled conditions. Focus is on measurable SLO impact, observable rollback criteria, and a reproducible runbook.

title: runtime-configurator-for-policy-based-retention-and-evidence-streaming
preview: Implement a runtime-configurable TTL allocator and evidence-streaming pipeline that derives retention for prompts, responses, request logs, and metrics from an authoritative policy set. The feature should support per-tenant, per-route, and per-region derivation, allow deterministic purge orchestration, and emit continuous evidence manifests to a region-aware archive for audits. This ticket covers the runtime components, API surface for policy overrides, streaming hooks for live evidence delivery, and operational rollback controls.

title: Redesign paged ranking envelopes and batchstream semantics for rerank API
preview: Problem: The current reranking endpoint mixes streaming, page cursors, and top_k semantics in ways that make client implementation error-prone for large result sets and causes inconsistent latencies for batched rerank workloads. This ticket redesigns the request/response envelope ("paged ranking envelopes") and introduces an explicit batchstream mode to decouple per-request latency from large-K retrievals. Goals: improve ergonomics for clients, make pagination and stable continuation tokens deterministic across reranker variants, and reduce tail latency for high-concurrency embedding+rerank pipelines.

title: Elastic priority tiers and cross-region spillover admission controller
preview: Design and implement an admission controller that combines elastic priority tiers with cross-region spillover to enforce SLOs under extreme load. Must support per-tenant priorities, dynamic demotion/promotion based on recent SLO burn, and graceful degradation modes (cache-first, context trimming, quantized-model fallback, canned stubs).

title: OpenAI Chat Completions compatibility orchestrator: replayable snapshot + request/response shape translator
preview: Problem: Customers calling Redwood with clients written for the OpenAI Chat Completions (CC) API expect identical request/response shapes, streaming chunk semantics, and deterministic function/tool calling behavior. We observe differences in: role/name field contractions, partial stream chunk boundaries for function calls, and non-deterministic toolcall attribution when we fall back to truncated model variants. This ticket implements an orchestrator that translates inbound OpenAI-CC requests into canonical Redwood runtime shapes, captures a replayable snapshot of request+prefix-state for deterministic replay (debugging/QA), and reconstitutes CC-compatible streaming and fn_call frames on the response path.

title: On-Call Baton Map & Contextual Responder Hub
preview: Design a lightweight, persistent "baton" visualization and accompanying "Responder Hub" surface that reduces context-switching during shift handoffs and incident escalation. The baton map should present a timezone-aware summary of current owners, last actions, and immediate next steps; the Responder Hub is a compact panel surfaced on alerts, timeline items, and the incident drawer that wires to postmortems, runbook snippets, and relevant SLO/cost deltas. Goals: speed up handoffs, reduce duplicate investigation, and make rollouts/alerts continuity explicit across shifts.

title: Cross-plane rapid regression failover and customer triage
preview:
Summary: During a staged rollout of runtime-1.19 model variant v2 we observed a correlated spike in tail latency and QA-quality regressions (hallucination score delta ~+12%) on a small subset of tenants across Hosted and Dedicated. This ticket coordinates a rapid cross-plane mitigation: immediate customer triage, canary quarantine, KV-cache reconciliation, and a staged hotpatch path that preserves tenant isolation for Private deployments.

Goals:
- Contain user-visible regressions within affected tenant slices without a broad rollback.
- Restore baseline quality & latency for high-visibility customers within a 4-hour window where possible.
- Produce an auditable rollback/hotpatch sequence and customer communication for post-incident RCA.

Scope: Hosted API, Redwood Dedicated pools, and Redwood Private (VPC) customers. Includes playbook steps for automated routing rules, NGINX/TLS frontdoor checks, KV-cache invalidation order, and canary state reconciliation across regional clusters.

Key technical trade-offs under discussion:
- Full rollback vs. slice quarantine: full rollback reduces blast radius but increases churn and cache cold starts (cost + transient latency).
- Live patch (hotpatch) to model weights/config versus traffic-split fallback to a conservative model: hotpatch is faster but riskier for unknown regressions in private deployments without immediate access.

Acceptance criteria:
- Containment procedure executed and validated for at least two affected tenant slices without customer-facing errors.
- Hotpatch PR merged and deployed to a canary pool with perf/quality gates green for 30m.
- Customer communications sent for all impacted customers with remediation ETA and mitigation steps.

Operational checklist (high-level):
1) Triage & classification: compile tenant list using /dashboards/model-rollout/alerts and mark Severity/Impact.
2) Pause rollout: update rollout-queue to stop additional traffic to v2.
3) Quarantine: use tenant-slice routing rules to pin affected tenants to runtime-1.18 (or pre-rollout pin) while preserving data residency for Private deployments.
4) KV-cache reconciliation: force prefix invalidation in order: hosted -> dedicated -> private (to avoid stale cross-tenant artifacts).
5) Patch path: deploy hotpatch binary (PR #9234) to isolated canary pool with explicit feature-flag off by default.
6) Verification: run the canary evalset + latency SLO monitors for 30m; run tenant-fast-check synthetic prompts.
7) Communication & RCA kickoff: issue customer bulletins and start RCA with cross-functional attendees.

Notes about Private deployments: Provide both a push-based hotpatch (for partner-managed on-prem) and a signed artifact that can be pulled by customer ops. Ensure KMS key compatibility before shipping artifacts.

title: Rationalize Terraform layering and accelerate plan execution
preview:
Background: Over the last 18 months we've accumulated several ad-hoc Terraform overlay patterns and tightly coupled provider initializations that slow down plan/apply runs and create frequent merge conflicts. This ticket scopes a focused refactor to rationalize the module layering, introduce a small compatibility shim for legacy overlays, and add parallelized plan execution and provider init improvements to reduce CI runtime and developer iteration time.

Goals:
- Split monolithic environment modules into clear logical layers (networking -> platform -> services) with well-defined inputs/outputs.
- Introduce a lightweight compatibility shim module to surface old overlay inputs while migrating consumers incrementally.
- Reduce terraform plan latency in CI by enabling targeted parallelization for independent workspaces and optimizing provider init (provider version pinning, partial init where safe).
- Upgrade AWS provider to 5.x and GCP provider to 5.x in a staged way with automated compatibility tests.
- Ensure state locking and migration steps do not cause downtime for Dedicated/private customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- New layer modules published to our internal module registry and consumable via module ref without breaking existing stacks.
- CI average plan time for infra repo reduced by >=35% on common PRs (measured over 10 PRs).
- Provider upgrades completed in staging with no plan diffs for critical production workspaces and documented rollback instructions.
- Compatibility shim in place with a migration checklist for consumer teams.
- Security review passed for provider version bumps and secrets handling changes.

Technical plan:
1) Design (3 days): map current module inputs/outputs and create a compatibility mapping doc. Link: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INFRA/Terraform+layering+map (referenced for reviewers).
2) Module split (5 days): implement networking-layer, platform-layer, services-layer modules. Each module will have explicit outputs for network ids, route tables, and region-aware outputs.
3) Shim module (2 days): provide shim that translates legacy overlay variables to new module inputs; mark deprecated variables with warnings.
4) Provider upgrade & init optimization (4 days): pin provider versions at module boundaries, add selective provider initialization in CI for independent workspaces and introduce provider init cache during local dev.
5) CI parallelization (3 days): modify pipeline to detect independent workspace changes and run terraform plans in parallel where safe.
6) Testing & rollout (4 days): automated integration tests, staged rollout: dev -> staging -> prod.
7) Post-rollout (2 days): monitor plan times, collect feedback, and iterate.

Rollback plan:
- If provider upgrade causes unexpected diffs, revert provider version pins in a targeted workspace and apply the last-known-good plan.
- Reintroduce legacy module composition using the shim if a consumer cannot migrate within the window.

Observability & metrics:
- Dashboard metric: terraform plan duration by PR and workspace. Goal: 35% reduction.
- Alerting: CI plan stage > 95th percentile threshold triggers ticket.

Risks:
- State layout changes could require manual migration steps for long-lived statefiles. We'll create a migration playbook per workspace.
- Provider upgrade may surface latent resource behavior changes; thorough staging tests required.

Links:
- RFC: https://docs.redwood.com/rfcs/terraform-layering-refactor
- Draft PR for module split: https://github.com/redwood/infra/pull/1234
- Related tickets: ENG-271831, ENG-433210

title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook
preview:
Problem: High-frequency burst traffic from a small set of tenants occasionally exhausts local quota tokens and triggers hard rejects or cross-region failovers. When the central limiter or its lease-store experiences transient outages, we currently fall back to a conservative deny-all behavior that leads to large SLO violations for affected customers. Goal: implement a resilient multi-tenant burst allocator that gracefully degrades under limiter outages and sustained overloads: - allow short, proportionally-weighted burst absorption using a token escrow backed by per-tenant standing reservations - introduce a soft-eviction policy to preempt low-priority work rather than hard reject high-priority requests - integrate with circuit-breaker signals so allocator behavior automatically widens or tightens depending on downstream health - provide deterministic admission behavior during global limiter outages by supporting a read-only fallback allocator with configurable safety caps - surface telemetry and alerts tied to SLOs and fairness regressions

Proposed solution summary: build an in-path Allocator service implemented as a stateless admission layer that consults a fast local lease cache and an eventual central bank for long-term accounting. Key mechanisms: 1) Escrow buffer: small, per-tenant short-term credits (milliseconds-to-seconds) that absorb microbursts. Credits decay and are replenished from the central bank at a bounded rate. 2) Weighted soft-eviction: when capacity is exhausted, the allocator preempts lower-priority requests (background embeddings, low-QoS retries) and returns a 202-driven defer response with retry hints instead of a 429 hard reject. 3) Emergency reserve and preemption order: reserve a configurable emergency pool for tenants tagged as SLA-critical; preemption order derived from tenant weight, recent usage, and emergency flag. 4) Circuit integration: allocator subscribes to regional circuit-breaker state; when downstream latency or error budget breaches occur, allocator shifts to aggressive preemption and tighter caps. 5) Read-only outage fallback: if central bank is unavailable, allocator uses a deterministic, conservative reservation algorithm (last-known quotas with decay) to avoid wide open fail-open. 6) Observability: fine-grained telemetry for per-tenant unfairness score, escrow consumption, preemption events, and fallback activation. 7) Chaos & sim-first rollout: validate in a simulator and run progressive chaos experiments before cluster rollout.

title: Q1 2026 perf baseline: kickoff, scope, and execution umbrella
preview:
## Why we’re doing this
We’re entering Q1 2026 with increasing variance risk: more regions, more GPU SKUs, and more customer traffic shapes (streaming + long-context + tool calling + embeddings). This umbrella tracks the end-to-end work to establish:

1) A repeatable benchmarking program (CI-backed) that is reproducible, attributable, and production-aligned.
2) A Q1 2026 “golden” baseline for top deployed models on key hardware/regions.
3) A representative workload suite v1 we can run in perf-canary + release gating.

Non-goal for this phase: external marketing benchmarks. This is internal baseline + release signoff tooling first.

## Deliverables (end of Q1)
- Baseline report (internal): model x region x GPU matrix + key takeaways + known cliffs + methodology
- Representative workload suite v1:
  - Chat streaming
  - Long-context prefill stress (summarization-like)
  - Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn)
  - Embeddings high-QPS
- Automated regression detection:
  - Nightly perf-canary runs
  - Gating mode on release branches
  - Noise model + thresholds + escalation playbook
- Reproducibility:
  - Canonical run-manifest schema + validator
  - Environment capture (driver/CUDA/runtime build, kernel selection, scheduler config)
  - Workload seeds + run naming conventions

## Scope (in)
### Runtime + instrumentation
- Scheduler policies (continuous batching heuristics, queue/backpressure/fairness, overload behavior)
- KV cache instrumentation & cache/cold-vs-warm measurement
- Kernel auto-selection telemetry and debug dumps (selected kernel + fallback reason)

### Benchmarking infrastructure
- Harness primitives + workload generators
- Run orchestration (repeatability, warmup, isolation steps)
- Results export format and dashboards

### Cross-functional alignment
- Applied ML/Evals: representativeness and model-variant comparability; minimal quality spot-check for perf runs
- SRE/Observability: production-aligned metrics definitions and alert routing hygiene

## Scope (out)
- Broad new model onboarding beyond what’s needed to include top models already in production
- Any customer-facing performance claims or public benchmark publications

## Baseline definition (what “Q1 2026 baseline” means)
- Baseline anchor = a pinned runtime build/tag for measurement and regression comparisons (see “Baseline freeze” below)
- We will report BOTH:
  - Cold path: caches off / empty
  - Warm path: prefix/KV cache enabled with defined warmup sequence

## Canonical metrics (high level)
- TTFT: time-to-first-token (streaming: first token on wire; non-streaming: first token produced, reported by server)
- TPOT: time-per-output-token (server-side; excludes client read bottlenecks)
- Throughput: tokens/s and/or req/s (workload dependent)
- Queue wait time + batch assembly time (to attribute tail latency)

(Exact definitions/measurement boundaries are documented in ADR-0172; see links.)

## Links (source of truth)
- Confluence plan (matrix + success criteria): sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/q1-2026-perf-baseline-plan.json
- Representative suite v1 definition: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/representative-workload-suite-v1.json
- Perf regression policy + thresholds standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds.json
- ADR-0172 (metrics + run manifest schema): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0172-benchmark-metrics-and-run-manifest-format.json
- ADR-0173 (workloads sourced from telemetry, privacy constraints): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0173-representative-workload-sourcing-from-prod-telemetry.json
- Scheduler baseline (Q1 2026 knobs/defaults): sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/scheduler-policy-baseline-q1-2026.json
- Kernel selection matrix (Q1 2026): sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/kernel-selection-matrix-q1-2026.json
- Profiling playbook: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/perf-suite-profiling-playbook.json
- Drive run tracker (baseline sweeps): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/serving-runtime/q1-2026-baseline-run-tracker.json
- Drive notes (open questions / variance): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/serving-runtime/q1-2026-perf-baseline-notes-wip.json

## Owners / working group
- Runtime umbrella: Mateo Alvarez (DRI)
- Benchmark harness: Rina Kobayashi
- Methodology / measurement: Claire Dubois
- Scheduling: Sofia Ivanova
- Kernels: Hiro Tanaka (with Yuki Sato for auto-selection stability)
- SRE integration + fleet hygiene: Sean Gallagher
- Dashboards + alerting: Sergio Costa / Aisha Bello
- Perf/evals program: Jonas Weber (w/ Dr. Maya Srinivasan sponsor)

## Milestones / checklist
### M0 — Kickoff + align on definitions (late Q4 2025)
- [x] Agree on workload taxonomy and target cohorts (streaming, long prefill, tool calling, embeddings)
- [x] Align on canonical metric boundaries (TTFT/TPOT/throughput; client vs server measurement)
- [x] Confirm privacy constraints + telemetry-derived distributions (ADR-0173)

### M1 — Harness primitives + reproducibility (early Q1 2026)
- [x] Run-manifest schema + validator landed (ENG-4822)
- [x] Standard warmup/isolation steps in harness
- [ ] Results schema versioning + backward compatibility story in docs

### M2 — Workload generators v1 (early–mid Q1 2026)
- [x] Chat streaming workload generator (ENG-4823)
- [x] Long-context prefill workload generator (ENG-4824)
- [x] Embeddings high-QPS mode (ENG-4825)
- [ ] Tool/function calling workload generator (NOTE: not yet a dedicated issue; will add if we can’t fold into ENG-4823)

### M3 — Runtime attribution instrumentation (mid Q1 2026)
- [x] Scheduler queue wait + batch assembly metrics (ENG-4826)
- [x] Kernel selection telemetry/debug dumps (ENG-4827)
- [ ] KV cache pressure counters (prefill/decode overlap + cache hit/miss tagging) — track follow-up issue

### M4 — Baseline sweeps + freeze (mid–late Q1 2026)
- [x] Initial sweep runs completed for us-east + eu-west on H100/A100 (L40S partial)
- [ ] Noise model calibrated; thresholds finalized per metric/workload
- [ ] Baseline freeze: finalize baseline anchor build/tag + lock kernel pinning exceptions

### M5 — Canary + gating + dashboards (late Q1 2026)
- [x] Nightly perf-canary mode running representative suite (ENG-4828)
- [ ] Gating mode on release branches with documented exceptions
- [x] Baseline dashboards with drill-down to manifests/traces (ENG-4829)
- [ ] Runbooks published (reruns, triage, escalation)

## Baseline freeze criteria (proposed)
We can freeze once:
- Per workload (streaming, long prefill, embeddings), we have at least N=30 runs per (model, region, SKU) for the baseline anchor to fit the noise model
- Known variance sources are controlled or annotated (GPU clocks locked where possible; container placement; warmup; cache state)
- Kernel pinning exceptions are documented (where auto-selection is unstable) with a plan to remove pins post-baseline

## Risks / open questions
- Variance: cluster contention / clock variability can make p99 noisy; need consistent isolation and a “noise budget” (Sana/Sean)
- Measurement boundary: streaming TTFT can be polluted by client read / network; we must enforce server-side timestamping for gating decisions
- Cache scenarios: reporting both cold and warm increases matrix size; dashboard must clearly differentiate
- Fleet capacity: baseline sweeps are GPU-hour heavy; ensure reservations match matrix commitments

## Next actions (this week)
- Mateo/Rina: finalize baseline freeze date + baseline anchor candidate tag
- Claire/Sana: publish noise model draft + recommended sample sizes for each workload
- Sean: confirm benchmark fleet isolation steps are enforceable in CI / nightly
- Sergio/Aisha: confirm alert routing (page vs triage channel) and add links from alerts -> manifest -> trace

title: Quantization hysteresis and batch interplay: long-warmup FP8/INT8 throughput & tail-latency study
preview: Background: Recent calibration runs for FP8/INT8 variants show non-monotonic throughput and tail-latency when we vary batch size and long-context windows. We suspect hysteresis in kernel selection + KV-cache warmup creates transient regions where inferred latency spikes and throughput reduces for a sustained period (tens of seconds) after a burst. Goal: produce reproducible microbenchmarks and end-to-end harness results that characterize (1) steady-state throughput across batch sizes and sequence lengths, (2) transient tail-latency after microbursts (warmup), and (3) points where kernel fallback triggers and causes accuracy/perf trade-offs. The results will feed a mitigation plan (runtime heuristics, calibration policy changes, and an autoscaler tuning doc).

title: SDK v1→v2 Migration: Streaming ergonomics, retry semantics, and changelog automation checklist
preview:
Summary:
This ticket captures a cross-language migration playbook for SDK v1 -> v2 focusing on: (1) streaming ergonomics changes (pull vs push, chunk framing, cancellation), (2) retry and idempotency semantics (header conventions, transient vs terminal errors, backoff policy), and (3) automated changelog/deprecation extraction to keep docs and migration guides in sync. The goal is a developer-friendly migration path that minimizes client breakage while enabling the cleaner v2 interfaces we shipped in the runtime.

Why this matters:
- Many customers embed streaming in UI flows; breaking ergonomics lead to hard-to-debug UI bugs.
- Retry semantics changed between lib versions: previously retries were best-effort on some codepaths; v2 centralizes retry policy in the transport layer which changes error surface and event ordering.
- We must ensure changelogs and deprecation notices are automatically surfaced to SDK docs and the Console so customers can plan upgrades.

Non-goals:
- Rewriting UI apps to use new streaming primitives (we provide examples and shims).
- Re-evaluating server-side semantics beyond what v2 runtime requires.

Checklist and recommended migration steps (high-level):
1) Audit call sites that use streaming endpoints (chat/generation/embeddings streaming). Flag any call sites that assume deterministic chunk sizes or synchronous ordering (these need explicit fixes).
2) Replace v1 streaming callbacks with the v2 streaming iterator pattern where possible; where not possible implement a small compatibility shim (see examples per language).
3) Add idempotency keys to mutation requests and follow new header format: X-RW-Idempotency-Key. Ensure serverside support for dedup window is tested.
4) Update retry behavior: use the runtime-provided RetryPolicy, prefer exponential backoff with jitter; limit to 3 attempts by default for non-idempotent requests and 5 for idempotent. Document these defaults in SDK changelogs.
5) Run changelog-automation tool to extract commits with scope sdk-* and footer BREAKING-CHANGE to generate a migration notes draft. Add manual edits for clarity.
6) Add CI job that runs the docs generator and posts a preview to the PR for each SDK release.

Language-specific migration snippets and notes:
Python (breaking ergonomics and shim recommendations):
- v1 pattern (callback-based streaming):
  def on_chunk(chunk):
    update_ui(chunk.text)
  client.stream_generate(prompt, on_chunk=on_chunk)

- v2 pattern (async iterator / context manager):
  async for event in client.stream.generate(prompt):
    update_ui(event.delta)

Migration shim (sync codebases): provide redwood.sdk.v2.compat.stream_to_callback
example:
  def stream_to_callback(async_iter, callback):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    async def _forward():
      async for e in async_iter:
        callback(e.delta)
    loop.create_task(_forward())

Notes: Python streaming now yields higher-level framing (events) instead of raw bytes. Tests need to be updated to assert events.sequence rather than arrival times.

TypeScript (ergonomics + cancellation semantics):
- v1: client.streamGenerate(prompt, onChunk) returns void; cancellation via opaque token.
- v2: client.stream.generate(prompt) returns AsyncIterable<StreamEvent> plus AbortSignal-aware controller. Example:
  const controller = new AbortController();
  for await (const ev of client.stream.generate(prompt, { signal: controller.signal })) {
    append(ev.delta);
  }
  // to cancel:
  controller.abort();

Migration tips: prefer using AbortController for deterministic cancellation. Provide a compat function streamToCallbacks(asyncIter, onChunk, onDone, onError). Ensure event ordering tests are updated to tolerate reordered telemetry from transient retries.

Go (sync and streaming goroutines):
- v1: StreamReceive(ctx, prompt, callback func(chunk string))
- v2: Stream(ctx, prompt) (chan StreamEvent, error) or iterator interface.

Migration shim: spawn goroutine that reads from channel and invokes legacy callback; propagate context cancellation immediately to reader to avoid goroutine leaks. Example snippet included in the docs folder.

Retry semantics changes (explicit):
- New default: SDK delegates to transport-level RetryPolicy. Clients should not implement their own high-level retry wrappers around streaming start calls.
- Idempotent endpoints (embeddings, read-only generation by prompt id) default to 5 attempts; non-idempotent (streaming sessions that alter server state) default to 1-3 attempts and require explicit idempotency key to raise attempts.
- Retries now emit telemetry events: sdk.retry.attempt and sdk.retry.final. Make sure CI tests that assert telemetry count are updated.
- Graceful partial-response handling: when a retry occurs mid-stream, SDK will emit an event of type "retry_start" and then re-emit the delta frames; consumers should treat deltas as append-only but not assume unique incrementing offsets unless using event.sequence metadata.

Changelog automation approach (technical):
- Conventions: commit scopes must be sdk-python, sdk-js, sdk-go, sdk-common. Breaking changes must include "BREAKING-CHANGE: <reason>" in commit footer.
- Tool: small node script (tools/extract-sdk-changelog.js) scans commits for the release range, groups by scope, and emits markdown blocks per language. It also pulls deprecation notes from decorators/annotations in source (eg @deprecated("v2.0.0", "Use new.foo") in TypeScript).
- Output: a per-language markdown file that is consumed by the docs generator; also attaches the summary to the GitHub release.

Testing matrix and CI updates:
- Add end-to-end smoke tests for streaming across Python, TS, Go with a synthetic prompt that simulates network flakiness (server injects transient 50x for first attempt). Verify retry telemetry, event ordering rules, and that compat shims behave identically to v1 in black-box tests.
- Add lint rule that fails PRs that change publicSurface without updating migration notes (compare exported symbols diffs).

Rollout plan:
- Phase 1 (Docs + Shims): publish migration guide and compatibility shims, bump minor release with opt-in flag (v2-preview).
- Phase 2 (Default v2 ergonomics): make v2 the default in SDK runtime on release day; publish automatic changelog and migration checklist to Console.
- Phase 3 (Deprecation): start deprecation timer for v1 endpoints (announce 6 months before removal).

Operational runbook for support engineers:
- How to detect issues: look for high counts of sdk.retry.final events or mismatched sequence numbers on Console traces.
- Quick mitigation: advise customers to apply compatibility shim (we have 1-line patch for each language) and escalate if they rely on server-side session persistence.

Outcomes and acceptance criteria:
- Migration guide published and accessible from SDK repo and docs.redwood.ai.
- Compatibility shims present and covered by unit + e2e tests.
- Changelog automation produces per-language artifacts on each release and attached to GitHub release.
- No regression in latency SLO for streaming endpoints in canary customers.

title: Burst capacity synthesizer for interactive spikes — proportional GPU headroom across weights/KV/activations
preview:
Problem: Interactive tails (short, high-QPS bursts) cause sudden KV and activation pressure that leads to tail latency spikes or evictions of hot weights on multi-tenant GPUs. Our current static headroom buckets are either too conservative (wasted capacity) or too aggressive (evictions under microbursts).

Goal: Design and implement a Burst Capacity Synthesizer (BCS) that creates a proportional ephemeral headroom fabric across weights, KV cache, and activations to safely absorb interactive spikes while minimizing steady-state waste. The synthesizer should integrate with continuous batching and the memory ledger so decisions are reversible and observable.

Approach summary:
- Build an Ephemeral Reserve Ledger (ERL) that tracks transient "burst tokens" per tenant at 1s resolution. Tokens are funded by forecasted slack and reclaimed automatically.
- Compute proportional reserves across three compartments: weights (pinned slots for model shards), KV (cache pages / prefix trees), activations (dynamic slab windows). The proportionality is derived from recent footprint ratios and a per-tenant SLA weight.
- Admission smoothing: integrate with the batching admission filter so that when ERL is depleted, new requests are throttled to the background queue or routed to quantized model variants.
- KV cold-window prefetch: on detected spike start, prefetch likely KV prefixes with a conservative budget (configurable via feature flags) to reduce cold-start misses while preserving headroom.

title: Chronicle Atlas: Micro-journey Mapping & Cost Lensing Prototype
preview: Goal: Prototype a compact, context-aware navigation overlay (Chronicle Atlas) that surfaces micro-journeys across Console surfaces (Dashboards, Rollouts, Cost Views) and folds in a lightweight cost-lensing control. The Atlas is intended to reduce cognitive load when switching contexts, surface relevant rollouts and cost metrics inline, and provide predictable routing behavior for multi-workspace customers. Scope: IA sketch -> mid-fidelity prototype -> internal usability session -> iterate on placement/interaction. Out of scope for this ticket: backend telemetry plumbing, RBAC enforcement, and Dedicated deployment config view changes (these are follow-ups tracked under ENG tickets).

title: Multiprotocol internal ingress terraform module: TLS passthrough + termination, cert fabric, health orchestration
preview: Create a single Terraform module that provides a multiprotocol internal ingress for Private deployments. The module must support: (1) NLB listeners for TLS passthrough to customer backends, (2) ALB for HTTP(s) termination where we need path/host routing and WAF integration, (3) dynamic certificate selection via ACM and Vault-stored certs (cert fabric), (4) configurable session affinity (cookie + source IP fallbacks), and (5) active health orchestration to minimize cross-AZ failover blast radius during upgrades. This ticket owns the module design, provider-agnostic Terraform code (AWS-first), integration tests, upgrade/rollout strategy, and automated healthcheck tooling hooks for Redwood Private packages.

title: Hotpatch and follow-ups for allocator fragmentation and session-cache corruption causing cross-environment GPU OOMs
preview: Summary: Multiple customers across Hosted, Dedicated and Private reported intermittent GPU OOMs over the last three weeks. Investigation shows two correlated runtime problems: (1) allocator fragmentation and small-allocation hoarding under long-lived model sessions, and (2) session-level in-memory cache corruption that prevents proper eviction and compaction, allowing KV state to grow without bound. Together these trigger OOMs on high-utilization nodes.

title: Compact clustered view and gesture-friendly column rails for large virtual tables
preview: Summary: Introduce a compact 'clustered' table density mode and a new gesture-friendly column-rail control to improve usability for very high-row-count views on touch and pointer devices. The clustered mode visually groups adjacent rows with small separators and semantic micro-headers to reduce perceived height while preserving scanability. The column rail is a narrow draggable affordance optimized for touch targets and momentum-aware resizing to avoid accidental full-width snaps. This ticket covers design tokens, component API surface, accessibility behavior, and handoff artifacts for engineering and PM.

title: Quant exception litmus run for FinSolve low-latency trading model
preview: Support ticket from customer FinSolve requesting an exception to allow FP8 quantization for their low-latency trading ranking model (llama-2-13b-custom) despite our default policy that prefers INT8 for this model family. Customer reports cost and latency constraints and requests a bespoke calibration run with their anonymized seed set and a short litmus evaluation to validate that accuracy stays within a 1.5% regression window on risk-sensitive signals. This ticket tracks engineering work to run the calibration, evaluate accuracy regressions on customer-provided and internal holdout sets, and produce a recommended quant-profile or a safe rollback policy. Deliverables: calibration artifacts, accuracy report, suggested quant-profile override YAML, support playbook for approval and follow-up.

title: Introduce export lineage tokens and TTL-based dedupe to eliminate transient duplicate invoice lines
preview:
Problem: Intermittent retry storms and race windows in the hourly export pipeline occasionally create pseudo-duplicate invoice lines in downstream Stripe/NetSuite exports. These duplicates are typically transient (same semantic usage but different export IDs or timestamps) and cause manual reconciliation work and customer confusion. Existing mitigations (watermarking and deterministic merge) have reduced frequency but do not prevent duplicates that emerge from out-of-order leader restarts, partial backfills, or multi-worker write races.

Goal: Add end-to-end lineage tokens to usage records produced by the ingestion tier and carry that token through aggregation and export. Use a TTL-based dedupe table in the export reconciler to detect semantically-equivalent lines within a configurable time window (default 48 hours). Ensure dedupe checks are idempotent and crash-recovery safe. Provide an opt-in dry-run mode for first rollout and strong observability (metrics and export-level flags) to measure false positives/negatives before enforcement.

Why unique: Instead of only relying on export fingerprints (hash of timestamp/account/amount), this change introduces a generated lineage token at produce-time and pairs it with a parity-key that tolerates acceptable timestamp discretization. The TTL-dedupe approach reduces the need for aggressive backfill suppression and supports safe local retries and replays from the ingestion queue.

Goal: Add end-to-end lineage tokens to usage records produced by the ingestion tier and carry that token through aggregation and export. Use a TTL-based dedupe table in the export reconciler to detect semantically-equivalent lines within a configurable time window (default 48 hours). Ensure dedupe checks are idempotent and crash-recovery safe. Provide an opt-in dry-run mode for first rollout and strong observability (metrics and export-level flags) to measure false positives/negatives before enforcement.

Why unique: Instead of only relying on export fingerprints (hash of timestamp/account/amount), this change introduces a generated lineage token at produce-time and pairs it with a parity-key that tolerates acceptable timestamp discretization. The TTL-dedupe approach reduces the need for aggressive backfill suppression and supports safe local retries and replays from the ingestion queue.

title: SLO fanout tolerance & proactive load-shedding dashboards for multi-region inference
preview:
Goal: Design and ship a set of instrumented SLIs and Grafana dashboards that enable proactive detection of multi-region capacity constraints and trigger gradient-based circuit breakers and targeted load-shedding before customer-visible SLO violations. Scope covers token-level latency SLIs, SLO fanout tolerance metrics per-customer shard, region capacity fingerprints, and a compact playbook for automated and manual mitigation.

Why this is needed: Current dashboards show high-level token p50/p95 but do not expose shard or fanout characteristics that lead to cascading failures during regional degradation. We need telemetry that correlates per-request fanout (replicas/streams), effective batching window, KV cache hit-rate, and tail token latencies so the control plane can make shedding decisions with low false positives.

Success criteria / acceptance criteria:
- Instrumentation: record token emission timestamps at source and sink, per-request fanout count, effective batch size, KV cache hit/miss at model shard granularity, and a deduplicated region capacity index. Metrics exposed as Prometheus-compatible counters/gauges/histograms.
- SLIs: implement token-latency histogram SLI that supports both latency-per-token and latency-per-generation percentiles (p50/p95/p99) with configurable rolling windows (1m/5m/1h). SLI should be taggable by region, model-family, customer_tier, and shard_id.
- Dashboards: five Grafana panels covering (1) fanout vs tail-latency heatmap, (2) shard saturation map with drift over 24h, (3) regional capacity fingerprint timeline, (4) circuit-breaker state and shed-rate by policy, (5) customer-impact map for active sheds. Dashboards must include links to playbook runbooks and the current canary config.
- Automation hooks: telemetry must emit a short JSON summary event to the control-plane topic (kafka://redwood.control.shedding) when pre-shed thresholds are crossed. Event schema and topic publishing are stubbed in this ticket (contract only).
- Observability QA: synthetic traffic scenario (see test plan) must reproduce a progressive capacity degradation and demonstrate automated gradational shedding with <5% false-positive sheds in 10 test runs.

Out of scope: full private/VPC deployment metrics ingestion (separate ticket), end-to-end quota billing integration (follow-up), and UI redesigns beyond dashboard panels.

Implementation notes and trade-offs considered:
- Histogram vs summary: we will use Prometheus histograms with preconfigured buckets to avoid quantile instability across cardinality; per-token histograms will be heavy so we will sample 1/10 requests by default and provide a toggle for higher fidelity on Dedicated customers.
- Tag cardinality: to limit cardinality we will restrict shard_id to shard_family (coarse buckets) and roll customer identifiers into customer_tier for dashboard aggregation. We will keep per-customer IDs for a separate low-cardinality audit stream.
- Sampling for production: default sample-rate 10% for token-level histograms, with adaptive ramp to 100% during incident-mode for affected regions. Sampling flags will be controlled via feature-flag service.

Acceptance artifacts to attach when done: PRs, Grafana dashboard JSON IDs, synthetic test logs (S3 link), control-plane event schema doc (Confluence link), and runbook snippet.

title: Cross-Backend Trace Delivery Coordinator with Partial-Ack and Replay Strategy
preview:
Goal: build a coordinator layer in the OTLP/ingest path that can deliver spans to multiple tracing backends (Tempo, Jaeger-compatible vendors) with graceful degradation and a partial-ack + replay strategy when downstreams fail.

Motivation: current single-sink writes lead to either blocking (latency spikes) or all-or-nothing failures and manual remediations. We need a mechanism that: prioritizes tenant lanes, records durable progress per-sink, acknowledges only what is durably stored, and enqueues failed envelopes for later replay to a cold DLQ (S3) while preserving ordering and idempotency guarantees required by our trace stitching and query paths.

Scope and non-goals: this ticket is focused on the coordinator and delivery semantics (ack model, replay) and light integration shims for Tempo/Jaeger endpoints. We are not reworking sampling policies, UI trace search, or long-term retention rules here.

High-level design decisions made:
- Partial-Ack model: on receipt, an envelope is assigned a delivery sequence; when a sink confirms durable write (or our durable buffer has flushed), we ack that sequence to the caller. This prevents blocking the ingestion pipeline for slow backends.
- Durable local buffer: small on-disk WAL per pod (configurable size) to allow short-term retries without relying solely on remote DLQ. Use local WAL + S3 DLQ for extended outages.
- Replay strategy: replay uses per-tenant priority lanes; low-priority tenants are rate-limited under pressure. Replay writes to sinks with backoff; unsuccessful replays after X attempts go to S3 DLQ.
- Idempotency: every envelope includes an idempotency token derived from (trace_id, span_id, envelope_seq, producer_epoch) so replays are safe and duplicates are de-duplicated by downstream adapters.
- Ordering: lightweight watermarking module that reorders spans where sequence gaps are detected up to a bounded window. Larger gaps are logged and moved to DLQ for investigation.

Trade-offs considered:
- Memory vs disk: local WAL reduces latency but increases disk I/O and storage pressure on pods; chosen default is modest (50MB) with operator-configurable thresholds.
- Consistency vs latency: partial-ack favors availability/latency at the cost of eventual consistency for the presence of spans in all backends; acceptable because querying layer has cross-backend stitching.
- Complexity vs operability: coordinator introduces stateful behavior per pod; mitigations: leaderless design and robust health checks so pods can be drained and state uploaded to S3 DLQ on termination.

Acceptance criteria:
- Coordinator routes to multiple sinks with per-sink success metrics exposed.
- Under a simulated sink outage, ingestion p50 increases by <10ms and p95 by <200ms for high-priority tenants in canary.
- DLQ artifacts contain sufficient metadata for replay and debugging.
- Feature flag + canary rollout plan exists and was executed in staging with 10k traces/sec synthetic workload.

title: Terraform PR gates + OPA/Conftest orchestration for Private VPC deployments
preview:
Summary:
We need a standardized, gateable CI workflow for all Terraform changes that affect Private (VPC) deployments. This ticket defines the PR-level plan/apply pipeline, the set of static checks (lint, tfsec/checkov), and the policy-as-code gating layer (OPA/Conftest). The goal is to ensure safe automated upgrades of Terraform modules, catch drift/unsafe network changes early, and allow deterministic rollout for Dedicated/Private customers.

Goals:
- Gate Terraform PRs with deterministic plan output and policy evaluations before any human approval.
- Prevent accidental open ingress, IAM over-permissive changes, or KMS misconfiguration from reaching prod Private deployments.
- Provide an automated upgrade job for module version bumps with canary apply and health-check rollback.
- Integrate drift detection and weekly health checks that surface via Console dashboards.

Design / Pipeline (proposed):
1) Pre-commit (local dev + CI): tflint, terraform fmt, terraform validate, checkov/tfsec quick scans. Fail fast on syntax/obvious security issues.
2) PR opened: CI executes terraform init + plan in isolated workspace; plan output is uploaded to artifact store and rendered as a structured diff.
3) Policy-as-code scan: Conftest (OPA rego) + optional Checkov policies run against the plan JSON. Policies include network rules (no 0.0.0.0/0 for private subnets), KMS key policies (no plaintext key rotation off), RBAC least-privilege patterns, and module version pins.
4) Policy results attached to PR with clear annotations; if any policy is 'deny' the PR enters Blocked state.
5) On approval, gated apply runs in a restricted runner tied to the customer's state backend with explicit approvals for sensitive resources (network, IAM, KMS).
6) Post-apply: run infra health checks (smoke tests + telemetry probe) and mark rollout success or trigger automated rollback.

Upgrade strategy for modules:
- Maintain a module registry with semver tags and changelogs.
- Weekly automated 'upgrade-candidate' job opens PRs for minor/patch upgrades with generated plan and policy scans.
- Major upgrades require opt-in and an integration test matrix against a Private sandbox (VPC) to catch behavioral changes.

Health & Drift checks:
- Scheduled Terraform plan against current state vs. desired state (nightly) to detect drift; drift alerts create an internal issue and run remediation playbook if safe.
- Liveness probes for critical resources (load balancer target health, routes, NAT gateway reachability) executed after applies.
- Metrics exported to Console: plan-failures, policy-denies, drift-detection-count, canary-failures.

Acceptance criteria:
- New PR template and pipeline are enforced for all repos with private-deployment Terraform modules.
- Conftest policy repo exists with at least 12 rego rules covering ingress, IAM, KMS, module pins.
- CI attaches human-readable policy annotations to PR diffs.
- One successful end-to-end upgrade PR run (minor module bump) with canary apply + automated rollback exercised in staging.

Risks / Tradeoffs:
- Strong gating may slow developer iteration; to mitigate, provide a fast path for non-sensitive changes (automated exemptions via labels + risk scoring).
- False positive policies may cause churn; we'll start with permissive warnings for a 2-week ramp before flipping denies for hard rules.

Artifacts & Links:
- Confluence RFC: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/TF+PR+Gates+and+Policy+Automation
- Policy repo (draft): https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-policies
- Example pipeline MR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-ci/pull/312
- Sandbox runbook: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/Private-Infra-Sandbox

Next steps / To do:
- [ ] Finalize initial rego rule set and get Security sign-off.
- [ ] Implement CI job templates (GitHub Actions + self-hosted runners) and test on infra-modules repo.
- [ ] Create monitoring dashboards for policy denials and drift alerts.
- [ ] Write docs for module owners on how to opt into exemptions and request manual review.

title: Compact NVTX naming and stream-linked kernel metrics for low-cardinality runtime traces
preview: Introduce a low-cardinality NVTX and span naming convention and attach CUDA stream-linked trace IDs so we can attribute per-kernel latency/CPU/GPU metrics back to user requests with minimal cardinality and overhead. Current NVTX labels are too verbose and high-cardinality (full op names + param suffixes) which floods exporters and makes aggregation expensive. This ticket designs and implements: 1) a compact canonical naming scheme mapping kernels to op families, batching mode, and quantization/precision; 2) propagation of a lightweight trace token onto CUDA streams so exemplar & span linking is possible for sampled requests; 3) a sampling strategy and reservoir to bound NVTX overhead for large requests; 4) exporter-side labels and histogram buckets for per-kernel latency, with exemplar trace IDs for p98/p99 latencies.

title: Introduce token-minting delegation service and audit-friendly token surface
preview:
Problem: We currently have a mix of long-lived root keys and ad-hoc service tokens that are hard to attribute in audit logs, difficult to rotate without customer impact, and incompatible with our SSO session model. This increases blast radius and slows incident response.

Goal: Design and implement a Token Minting & Delegation Service (TMDS) that issues short-lived, scope-limited tokens with machine- and human-readable metadata for auditability. TMDS should integrate with SSO/SAML sessions, support programmatic delegation (service->service), expose an admin surface for emergency revocation, and emit structured audit events compatible with existing logging pipelines.

Success criteria: (1) All new programmatic tokens are minted via TMDS by default, (2) Audit records show token provenance (issuer, parent token, SSO session id, requested scopes), (3) We can rotate underlying signing keys without immediate app-level downtime, (4) A migration path exists for high-risk long-lived keys to be replaced or escrowed.

title: Retire dual-write flag pipeline and canonicalize rollout timeline
preview:
Background: Over the last 3 years we introduced a dual-write pipeline that writes feature flag and rollout timeline changes to both the legacy 'compat' store (compat-rollouts) and the newer canonical rollout_store. The compat store exists to support legacy admin endpoints and older SDKs. Keeping both writers has increased operational complexity (double failure modes), caused divergent state during partial failures, and slowed migration of rollout history queries.

Goal: Stop dual-writing, migrate any missing historical timeline rows into rollout_store, remove the compat-adapter codepath, and decommission the compat-rollouts dataset. After completion, all reads should be served from rollout_store and the legacy endpoints should return 410 or proxy to new endpoints per the API migration plan.

Success criteria (high level):
- Backfill job migrated all historical timeline entries for 99.9% of organizations (with a manual audit for the remainder).
- No read traffic hits compat-rollouts for 14 consecutive days in canary region.
- Compat adapter binary removed from repo and no feature flags reference the legacy store.
- Admin UI and SDKs switched to canonical endpoints and tests updated.
- Monitoring dashboards show no increase in rollout-query latency; error budget impact <= 0.5% during migration window.

Risks & mitigations:
- Large timeline objects: discovered some orgs have >2GB aggregated timeline blobs. Mitigation: chunking in the backfill job and incremental reassembly in the migration verification.
- Race conditions during cutover: plan a two-phase switch (reads-only proxy -> read-from-canonical -> disable dual-write). Canary traffic and a supervised ramp help detect regressions.
- Audit/legal retention: ensure admin audit logs are snapshotted and preserved in S3 before deleting compat dataset.

Rollout plan (summary):
1) Read-only proxy: route reads from compat endpoint to rollout_store while keeping legacy endpoint available behind a compatibility shim.
2) Backfill: run throttled backfill job that copies missing timeline rows and compacts duplicates into canonical schema. Job writes progress checkpoints and supports resume.
3) Canary: enable read-from-canonical for 5% of orgs for 48 hours, monitor errors/latency.
4) Switch: flip routing for all reads to canonical store and disable dual-write.
5) Cleanup: deprecate compat endpoints, remove adapter code, delete compat dataset after snapshot.

Operational notes: Backfill job should use per-org rate limits and exponential backoff. Avoid scanning entire compat dataset in a single pass. Use a cursor-based approach keyed by org_id and last_seq. Include hooks to surface problematic orgs to the oncall team for manual remediation.

- Large timeline objects: discovered some orgs have >2GB aggregated timeline blobs. Mitigation: chunking in the backfill job and incremental reassembly in the migration verification.
- Race conditions during cutover: plan a two-phase switch (reads-only proxy -> read-from-canonical -> disable dual-write). Canary traffic and a supervised ramp help detect regressions.
- Audit/legal retention: ensure admin audit logs are snapshotted and preserved in S3 before deleting compat dataset.

Rollout plan (summary):
1) Read-only proxy: route reads from compat endpoint to rollout_store while keeping legacy endpoint available behind a compatibility shim.
2) Backfill: run throttled backfill job that copies missing timeline rows and compacts duplicates into canonical schema. Job writes progress checkpoints and supports resume.
3) Canary: enable read-from-canonical for 5% of orgs for 48 hours, monitor errors/latency.
4) Switch: flip routing for all reads to canonical store and disable dual-write.
5) Cleanup: deprecate compat endpoints, remove adapter code, delete compat dataset after snapshot.

Operational notes: Backfill job should use per-org rate limits and exponential backoff. Avoid scanning entire compat dataset in a single pass. Use a cursor-based approach keyed by org_id and last_seq. Include hooks to surface problematic orgs to the oncall team for manual remediation.

title: Region-aware fallback graph and automated deprecation workflow for pinned model versions
preview:
Problem statement:
Customers can pin to a model version (e.g., gpt-ax.v1) for stability. Today, when a pinned model becomes unavailable in a region (out of capacity), is deprecated, or fails runtime checks, behavior is inconsistent: some requests error, some fall back to unrelated variants, and others silently switch to smaller models causing quality regressions. This causes production incidents, confused customers, and support load.

Goal:
Design and implement a deterministic, configurable fallback graph and an automated deprecation workflow for pinned versions. The system should: 1) prefer region-local compatible variants first, 2) respect customer pinning semantics (allow explicit opt-in to automatic downgrade), 3) surface deprecation notices and allow staged auto-migrations, and 4) integrate with published performance profiles so fallback choices minimize unit-cost and quality regressions.

Scope (this ticket):
- Define fallback graph ordering and policy primitives (region preference, variant affinity, quantization compatibility, and cost/latency thresholds).
- Add a new pinning policy schema (fallback_policy) to the model pin API and console.
- Implement runtime routing changes to evaluate fallback_policy at request routing time.
- Deprecation automation: signals, notification cadence, and optional auto-migration after X days.
- Publish changes to performance-profile docs so console/SDK can surface expected trade-offs for fallbacks.

Out of scope for this ticket:
- New pricing changes, per-tenant legal notice flows, and large-scale data migrations for already-pinned customers (those will be handled in follow-ups).

title: Heuristic orchestrator for kernel routing using GPU architecture and sequence-length fingerprints
preview:
Problem: The runtime currently picks a single preferred kernel per model variant that doesn't account for GPU architecture differences (SM count, tensor core availability), or runtime characteristics like sequence length distributions for prefill vs decode. This results in suboptimal decisions: high-seqlen prefill on Ampere GPUs picks kernels tuned for short sequences, and decode paths with KV-cache memory patterns pay a latency penalty.

Objective: Implement a lightweight heuristic orchestrator that routes inference requests to the most appropriate kernel variant based on a compact GPU \"fingerprint\" and sequence-length bucket (prefill vs decode). The orchestrator should be fast (microsecond-scale decision), safe (fallback to conservative kernel on uncertainty), and observable (clear telemetry for kernel choices and outcome).

Key ideas and constraints:
- GPU fingerprinting: capture a small set of architecture attributes at agent startup: compute capability, SM count, tensor core capability, peak fp16/INT8 throughput, and L2 size. Avoid full benchmarking on cold start to keep agent startup < 10s.
- Sequence-length bucketing: separate decision logic for prefill (longer seqs, large memory writes) vs decode (1..n tokens with heavy KV cache reads). Define buckets: short (<=64), medium (65-512), long (>512) with a tunable config.
- Kernel candidates: maintain a small registry per model-version containing 2-4 vetted kernel variants (e.g., small-seqlen-optimized, large-seqlen-optimized, tensorcore-tiling, memory-friendly). Kernels are indexed by heuristic tags.
- Cost model: runtime estimator uses a cheap cost function (expected token throughput and an estimated latency penalty) to pick kernel. Preference can be tuned per-customer: low-latency vs high-throughput.
- Safety & adaptivity: prefer offline microbenchmarks (CI-generated) to pre-populate registry; at runtime allow a short sampling phase (10-30 requests) to confirm choice. If sampled latency deviates > 15% from expected, mark kernel as degraded and switch. Telemetry should capture sampling outcomes to inform offline retraining of heuristics.
- Integration points: kernel registry (metadata store), scheduler (routing decision), and telemetry pipeline. Decision must be serializable and cacheable per (model-version, GPU-fingerprint, seqlen-bucket).

Acceptance criteria:
- Runtime selects different kernels for at least two GPU classes (e.g., Ampere vs Hopper) and different seqlen buckets in canary experiments, with measurable improvement: >=10% latency reduction p50 for targeted buckets or >=12% throughput increase for throughput-focused policy.
- Decision latency < 200us in the hot path.
- Robust fallback path: no request fails due to kernel pick logic alone.
- Telemetry exposes kernel_choice, expected_latency, observed_latency, sample_count, and decision_reason.

Out-of-scope for this ticket:
- Full ML-driven autotuner (future ticket). This work is focused on a lightweight heuristic and a small online sampling loop.

Deliverables:
- Lightweight GPU fingerprint module (agent) + unit tests.
- Seqlen bucketing and decision API (C++ runtime + Python bindings).
- Kernel registry schema extension for heuristic tags.
- Canary deployment plan and perf harness adjustments.
- Dashboard panels showing kernel routing by model, region, and GPU family.

title: streamline-sdk-dependency-graph-and-remove-legacy-promise-shims
preview: Goal: Reduce maintenance cost and runtime risk by consolidating SDK dependency versions across Python, JS, and Go clients, removing legacy promise/callback shims in the JS client, and cleaning up deprecated API surface. This ticket coordinates library bumps, compatibility shims, migration docs, CI changes, and rollout strategy. Scope: (1) Bump supported transitive deps across SDKs (httpx, aiohttp, node-fetch -> undici, protobuf, gogo/protobuf replacements), (2) Remove legacy promise/callback wrappers in JS client and expose unified async/await + synchronous helper where possible, (3) Run full integration matrix against hosted API and dedicated runtime to detect regressions, (4) Publish migration guides and codemods, (5) Prepare canary and gradual rollout plan with feature flags, (6) Update packaging and release tooling (build matrices, pinned GH actions). Out of scope: Large API surface changes to server API. Deprecation windows will be communicated in patch/major cadence.

title: Warm-Standby Certification & Region Handoff Orchestrator (hopscotch method)
preview: Goal: Implement an orchestrator and certification pipeline that exercises multi-region failover semantics and validates warm-standby readiness using a staggered 'hopscotch' priming pattern to avoid synchronized cache churn. This ticket covers: (1) a controller that coordinates region selection and tier routing decisions during outage windows, (2) an automated readiness scoring service that runs periodic probes and computes a single readiness_score per region+tier+model_variant, (3) an acceptance/test harness to certify warm standby pools before they are accepted as active failover targets, and (4) telemetry and alerting to detect degraded priming or variant incompatibilities. Context: previous warm-start efforts experienced synchronous cache invalidations when all standby pools followed the same priming cadence. The hopscotch method staggers priming across regions and model variants so at least one candidate remains cache-hot while others are being refreshed. This reduces client-visible latency spikes during cross-region handoffs and improves success rate of automatic failovers. Scope: orchestrator logic + readiness probe definitions + certificate issuance for standby pools + rollout plan and runbook updates. Out of scope: changes to model training or format conversions; separate ticket will handle model-variant packaging if needed.

title: Length Huddle: token-aggregator for runtime pad-shedding and microbatch consolidation
preview: Implement a runtime-level 'Length Huddle' token-aggregator that groups concurrent requests into transient micro-huddles by sequence-length proximity and request phase (prefill vs decode), to reduce padding and improve kernel utilization without adding significant scheduling overhead. The feature targets short-to-mid tail workloads where padding dominates token cost and kernel occupancy is suboptimal.

title: Welcome Companion: adaptive setup sequencer and contextual playbooks
preview: Introduce a lightweight in-product ‘Welcome Companion’ that adaptively surfaces a short sequenced setup experience based on user signal (account age, first API calls, chosen use case). The companion combines three UI primitives: a small persistent coach panel, contextual tooltips anchored to relevant console surfaces (dashboards, rollouts, cost views), and a library of one-click seed templates. Primary goals: reduce time-to-first-success, teach cost controls, and prevent confusion in empty states without adding modal friction.

title: Tenant embedding shift detector for zero-day workload changes
preview: Build an online detection service that represents tenant-level usage/cost/latency time-series as compact embeddings and surfaces zero-day workload shifts (novel cluster formation or re-assignment) with probabilistic alert scoring and noise-aware suppression. Goal: catch new behavioral regimes (engineered model changes, synthetic traffic, customer load pattern shifts) faster than threshold-based rules while keeping false positive rate low for on-call and product owners.

title: Automatic SLI Derivation and Cost-Constrained Trace Capture for Analytics Ingest
preview:
Problem: Our analytics ingest pipeline produces a wide variety of per-tenant and per-route signals (ingest latency, parse errors, queueing depth, drop rates). Instrument owners keep manually authoring SLIs and choosing sampling policies for traces when things go wrong. That process is slow, error-prone, and leads to poor coverage during short-lived hotspots.

This ticket implements two coordinated capabilities: (1) automatic SLI derivation (ASD) that proposes candidate SLIs from existing metrics and synthetic probes using heuristics and supervised rules, and (2) cost-constrained trace capture (CCTC) — an adaptive, budgeted trace sampling controller that reserves trace capture for signals that matter to derived SLIs and anomalies. The goal is faster SLI onboarding, better signal-to-noise for incidents, and bounded observability cost for analytics ingestion services.

title: Cross-model streaming and tool-call semantics validation for pinned model variants
preview: Objective: Validate that every newly onboarded model (and pinned model variants) preserves streaming semantics and deterministic tool/function-calling behavior when published with a performance profile. Scope: run a cross-model test matrix that exercises streaming partial outputs, mid-stream function/tool call frames, cancellation/retry, and the interaction with quantized/optimized kernels implied by published perf profiles. Deliverables: 1) Test harness integrated into CI that can run per-model streaming + tool-call scenarios; 2) A compatibility matrix (Confluence) with pass/fail and mitigation notes; 3) A canary rollout plan and a regression alert tied to the existing eval suite; 4) PRs that gate model onboarding until passing results are present. Acceptance criteria: - For each model variant in the onboarding batch, streaming must preserve exact token ordering for prefix+stream sequences in at least 99/100 synthetic sessions. - Function/tool-call frames must be emitted as a single, well-formed event (not split across streaming chunks) or, if streamed incrementally, must include clear boundary metadata per our streaming protocol. - No silent truncation of function arguments when kernel quantization or KV cache path changes are applied by the perf profile. - Latency regression vs benchmark profile must be within the defined threshold (<20% of profile SLO) for pinned variants in staging. - Canary traffic must show no more than 0.5% error-rate increase and no high-severity customer-impacting differences in outputs as measured by eval suites. - Concurrency/batching stress test (1000 concurrent sessions in staging dedicated pool) should not cause re-ordered function-call events. Out of scope: model architecture-level fixes (those are part of model ingestion work), long-tail corner cases unrelated to streaming/tool-call (to be captured in follow-ups).
